text:
{
 "prose": "Across the width of the lamp room, past the great turning lens, Elias catches Maren's eye and holds it. He does not say anything. He has said enough tonight, and so has she, and the lamp room at this hour does not want for more words than it already has.

Instead he nods, once, slow and deliberate, the way a man nods to close a bargain rather than open one.

She understands it anyway, the way she has come to understand most of what he does not say aloud. Whatever she chooses when the survey ship returns and dawn comes up gray over the shoals, she will not be choosing it alone on this rock anymore, turning it over by lamplight with only the ledger and her grandmother's cramped hand for company. He will be standing where he is standing now, close enough to touch, when she has to decide.

It is not an answer. It is not even a promise, not quite, not in so many words. But it is the shape of one, and after three generations of keepers who kept this watch by themselves, Maren finds it is enough to let her breathe out something she had not known she was holding.

Below them, faint through the iron and the stone, the sea works at the stilts the way it always does, indifferent to what has just passed between two people standing in borrowed lamplight, waiting out the last hours before the tide turns.
",
 "micro_details": []
}
